[[RunningHead]]

A Working Poet Comments on Caston's “Poetic Closure, Psychoanalytic Termination, and Death”

Irene Willis

Irene Willis

2 Cornwall Drive
Great Barrington, MA 01230-1592
E-mail: irenej@vgernet.net

Irene Willis   Author of two collections of poetry: They Tell Me You Danced (University Press of Florida, 1995) and At the Fortune Café (Snake Nation Press, 2005).

A poem is never finished, only abandoned.

—Paul Valéry

Why is a poem abandoned? Not because to end it would feel like death, but because there is no such thing as real closure in poetry. We may declare a poem “finished,” meaning that is the best we can do with it for now, and send it out into the world that way. Twenty years later we may do something more with it, revise it in some way, even after publication. Sometimes we abandon a poem because the conflict in it has not been resolved. A good poem often contains contradictions, emotions that seem to cancel each other out. The resolution of the irreconcilable may be the objective of the poem, and the music and imagery of the poem play with that.

The problems that the poet attempts to resolve, however, are aesthetic, not personal. The poet is not at the mercy of or manipulated by the images used in the poem; rather, she may be drawing upon the materials of the self, much as a visual artist or sculptor would use clay or stone or paint to create forms and compositions. Poetry, even at its most “confessional,” is not psychoanalysis; nor, even at its most cathartic and therapeutic for the poet, is it psychotherapy.

43

[[RunningHead]]

III. Dimensions and New Measures Relevant to Psychoanalysis

Development of the Dyadic Reflective Functioning Questionnaire (DRFQ)

David Younger

David Younger

Philoctetes Center
350 West 57th Street # 9B
New York, NY 10019
E-mail: dbyounger@gmail.com

David Younger  

The principal aim of this project is to create a measure to assess dyadic reflective function in couple relationships—the ability to put oneself into one's partner's shoes—and to explore whether different degrees of theory of mind are related to, and are predictive of, attachment style security and relationship satisfaction.

Background

Reflective function, according to Fonagy and Target (1997), is a product of development whereby the child not only learns to respond to other people's actions, but to how he or she conceives of them. The notion of reflective function has its foundation in Freud's concept of bindung, or linking, and in Dennett's thesis (1978, 1987) on predicting behavior. Reflective function is an inherently interpersonal process whereby an individual organizes his or her internal world according to the behavior and assumed mental states of others.

It has been demonstrated that the attachment system (Bowlby 1969, 1973, 1980) is directly connected to the capacity for reflective function and development of the self. Secure attachment allows for the freedom and security to develop the ability to think about what the other is going to do. Disorganized attachment, possibly as a result of trauma, directly affects the development of reflective function.

318

Nancy Wolf

Nancy Wolf

666 West End Avenue, Suite 1A
New York, NY 10025
E-mail: nhwolfrsd@aol.com

Nancy Wolf   Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Freudian Society.

The contributors to this volume of the Annual address Freud's theories on women through the varying disciplines of psychoanalysis, cultural theory, and political theory. The articles are grouped into four categories: Clinical, Theoretical, Personal Narratives, and Women Who Shaped Psychoanalysis. The editors conclude that the essential changes in thinking about women have been in the significance accorded relatedness rather than autonomy, in the attention given the vicissitudes of development (including but not privileging the oedipus complex), and in the emphasis on culture as a shaper of the female psyche. As the editors state, “Psychoanalytic psychology of women is richer and better developed… but… very much a work in progress”; this volume, they write, is “a contribution to that ongoing enterprise” (p. 5).

The first section, on clinical work, has essays by Joyce McDougall and Lynne Layton. McDougall's “The Psychoanalytic Voyage of a Breast Cancer Patient” describes her sensitive understanding, holding, and interpretive work with Sorel, a forty-year-old woman in the midst of treatment for breast cancer. In their work together, Sorel begins to understand that she has internalized a lethal mother introject and that her attacks upon her body, preceding her diagnosis, were ways to annihilate this unconsciously fantasized deathly intruder. It is not so much the cancer that brings Sorel to McDougall, for she has long ago resigned herself to death, but her terror of her loneliness in the sterile room she will be in for a bone marrow transplant. With McDougall, she begins to recognize the sterility of her childhood and her configuration of this loneliness into a somatic delusion of a black mass within.

The Internal World and Attachment. By Geoff Goodman. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 2002, 339 pp., €57.50.

Ronnie Shaw

Ronnie Shaw

4900 Cherry Creek South Drive, Suite H
Denver, CO 80246
E-mail: rshaw@ashcomm.com

Ronnie Shaw   Faculty, Denver Institute for Psychoanalysis; Associate Clinical Professor, Department of Psychiatry, University of Colorado School of Medicine.

This is an ambitious and passionate attempt to do something that few would try: to bridge the gap between attachment theory and psychoanalytic theory. Goodman's scrupulously researched and written text is a manifesto of his deeply held opinion that these competing theories are not only compatible but should be used by clinicians in an integrated fashion to inform technique and better serve treatment and research. Goodman, who has trained at acclaimed institutions and is a psychoanalytic researcher with a specialty in the area of attachment, has twice received the Traditional Plumsock Prize for his work. If anyone were to succeed in demonstrating the usefulness of this idea, Goodman would seem to be the ideal candidate. In actuality, he has produced a very detailed work to support the concept that the two theoretical frameworks are conceptually related.

The author readily admits his task is ambitious. Stating that “perhaps the most controversial point of contention between psychoanalytic theory and attachment theory is their theories of motivation” (p. 4), he boldly adopts a drive theory of motivation in an attempt to integrate object relations thought and attachment theory. But even within the two fields, the theory of motivation is controversial. Goodman observes that some object relations theorists acknowledge a drive theory of motivation, while others do not. He goes on to offer his understanding of the controversy. Quoting Fonagy's statement that “the notions of drives and psychic agencies … have no place in an attachment theory framework” (1999, p. 613), he counters by saying “such an attitude might obscure the merits of a drive theory of motivation.… No one I know experiences himself or herself as a thermostat that turns on or off depending on the environment” (p. 5). Summing up his position, Goodman writes that “attachment can be viewed as a system or a drive, depending on one's point of view. If the interaction between the organism and the environment is being studied, then applying a systems theory to understand the observed phenomena seems warranted. If, instead, the internal structures of the mind are being studied, then applying a drive theory to understand the phenomena seems likewise warranted.… In this book the focus always is on the internal world and its development” (p. 6).

The Psychoanalysis of Science: the Role of Metaphor Paraprax, Lacunae and Myth. By Yehoyakim Stein. Brighton: Sussex Academic Press, 2005, vii + 190 pp., $67.50.

Lois Oppenheim

Lois Oppenheim

181 East 73rd Street
New York, NY 10021
Fax: 212-988-9684
E-mail: oppenheimL@mail.montclair.edu

Lois Oppenheim   Distinguished Scholar, Professor of French, and Chair, Department of Modern Languages and Literatures, Montclair State University; candidate, Scholars Program, New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

When psychoanalysis is applied to art, it aims to uncover something about a particular work or artistic oeuvre. Alternatively, it seeks to reveal something about the creative impulse that gave rise to it. The application of psychoanalysis to science poses a very different set of issues, and though the topic is intriguing, one wonders whether in this book Yehoyakim Stein has embarked on a route hitherto little traveled (if ever) for at least one very good reason. In a word, psychoanalysis is, phenomenologically speaking, the analysis of the psyche. Hence, the all too often repeated criticism of the application of psychoanalytic thinking to art—namely, that there is no live input from the artist, nothing comparable to the freely associative mind of a patient that is the sine qua non of the analytic endeavor. Yet art, unlike science, makes no claim to objectivity, and that is precisely where the fault line of the psychoanalysis of science may well reside.

Stein addresses the difficulty head on: he makes the claim from the start that science is more subjective than it knows itself to be, that it is both driven by unconscious motivations and hindered in its development by blind spots and lacunae of various kinds. So far the question of the application of psychoanalysis to science would appear credible. After all, there is creativity in science, and creativity is necessarily subjective.

724

Stephen Sternbach

Stephen Sternbach

1105 Massachusetts Avenue 1-C

Stephen Sternbach   Faculty, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Clinical Instructor of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School.

At one level of description, Freud's theory of the oedipus complex involves a drama that each child experiences within the family. In-evitable and ineluctable, the oedipus complex was for Freud also something more, the experience of a passage the individual must undergo in one manner or another to become human in a social order. For Freud, this passage has all the weight of fate invoked in the original myth; as such, the oedipus complex attains the status of a structure.

727

Freud's Wizard: Ernest Jones and the Transformation of Psychoanalysis. By Brenda Maddox. DaCapo Press, 2007, 368 pp., $26.00.

George Moraitis

George Moraitis

232 Pinehurst Avenue
East Longmeadow, MA 01028

George Moraitis   Former Training and Supervising Analyst, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis.

Brenda Maddox is a well-known biographer who has entered the crowded field of the history of psychoanalysis with her new book, Freud's Wizard: Ernest Jones and the Transformation of Psychoanalysis.

Although not an analyst herself, Maddox is quite familiar with the psychoanalytic world. She claims that, for her, “one attraction of

1423

Jones was the way his life touched the others about which I have written” (p. 2). These others are Nora (Mrs. James) Joyce, D. H. Lawrence, W. B. Yeats, and Rosalind Franklin, the “dark lady” of DNA. Ernest Jones had, of course, touched the lives of almost all psychoanalysts of his time and many others since then. He introduced psychoanalysis in England, facilitated its development in all English-speaking countries, and was for many years the most powerful person in the psychoanalytic world. His biography of Freud established him as “the principal historian of the psychoanalytic movement” (p. 268).

Writing the biography of such a man—and with a psychoanalytic readership in mind—must have been, for Maddox, a daunting task. She has succeeded, however, in overcoming many obstacles and producing an impressive biographical study of Jones's rise in the world of psychoanalysis with her diligent study of voluminous documents, which include, but are by no means limited to, Jones's personal correspondence with Freud (671 letters) and his extensive publications. In addition, Maddox interviewed a number of people, such as Jones's sons and daughter, consulted with many others, and systematically researched records pertinent to her subject's activities.

In intellectual history, the biographer must integrate at least three stories into one: the story of the subject's personal life, the story of the development of the subject's creativity, with its ups and downs, and the story of the social and intellectual environment within which the subject's life and creativity took place.

The aim of the biographer is to do justice to all three stories, but all too often one of them receives a privileged position in the narrative. Maddox has placed the emphasis on Jones's relationship with Freud, as the title of the book indicates. Her choice is understandable, given the fact that Jones lived most of his life in Freud's shadow.

Comings and Goings

Steven Levy

Steven Levy

Steven Levy  

Another year has passed and it is again time to give thanks and bid farewell to an inspired group of editorial readers who have helped maintain JAPA's high standards while pushing us into new territories of inquiry and scholarship. Good-bye and heartfelt thanks to Sidney Blatt, Henry Evans, Lewis Kirshner, Lucy LaFarge, Janice Lieberman, Calvern Narcisi, Sidney Phillips, Alan Skolnikoff, and Leon Wurmser. Welcome as well to new board members Lee Ascherman, Judith Chused, Peter Dunn, Dianne Elise, Gerald Fogel, Peter Goldberg, Linda Mayes, Donald Moss, and Richard Munich. I am certain that they will cherish, as I do, the opportunity to help authors move our field forward, and will be enriched by exchanges with fellow board members and editorial staff in the never ending work of the journal. I trust that being an integral part of JAPA will be a career highlight for them, as it has been for me and for all those colleagues who have contributed in the past. It is a heady group to belong to. And then there is the matter of ending a sentence with a preposition!

5

Stuart Hauser  

In this research section we present summaries of the final fifteen posters from the large and successful Fifth Annual Poster session. Readers will find two groups of summaries—II. Human Development: Intersections with Psychoanalytic Perspectives; and III. Dimensions and New Measures Relevant to Psychoanalysis. These summaries represent the rich array of posters presented in January 2006. The first group of presentations—I. New Studies of Psychoanalytic and Psychodynamic Psychotherapy: Conceptual, Process, and Outcome Studies—was published in JAPA 54/4.

As word of our openness to psychoanalytically relevant theoretical and empirical contributions has spread to colleagues in other fields, they have become increasingly aware of this new opportunity for working with psychoanalytic ideas and disseminating their work. The Poster Session has greatly benefited from this networking, having received a record number of rigorous, thoughtful, and original presentations in 2006. This welcome trend continues, with submissions to the 2007 Poster Session exceeding by a third those of the previous year.

I expect readers will be delighted, and intellectually stimulated, by the new ideas, directions, interpretations, and research methods they will find here. Deliberately, the summaries present works in progress—the beginnings of longer papers, and of new programs of research. To allow authors to more clearly communicate the conceptual and empirical complexity of their work and to argue for its wider implications, we encouraged all to go beyond the more usual (and telegraphic) single-page abstract.

239

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Inspiring Inquiries: Introduction to Falkenström Et Al.

Stuart Hauser

Stuart Hauser

E-mail: shauser@jbcc.harvard.edu

Stuart Hauser  

Two admirable strengths characterize the following very original paper by Frederik Falkenström and his colleagues. First, the topic—continuing development following psychoanalysis or long-term psychotherapy—is rarely addressed using empirical research designs. Second, they have carried out a bold and outstanding inquiry, guided by a commitment to apply complementary narrative and quantitative methods in rigorously and deeply tracking the changing lives of former psychotherapy patients for two years following termination.

Analysts and psychotherapists have long been aware of changes— sometimes stunning, sometimes subtle—that patients initiate and experience at various points after formal completion of their therapy. Sometimes we learn of these changes when a patient returns for a follow-up consultation. At other times we hear about, or perhaps even witness, continuing or new development in close friends and significant others. And in another personal way, we learn about these surprising moments and patterns through self-observation, at times years after completing our own therapy or analysis. It has been a challenge to identify these changes and then discover the mechanisms underlying them. What proximal and distal triggers leading to growth or deterio-ration may possibly be linked with prior long-term psychodynamic therapy or psychoanalysis? To what extent do changes after termination reflect continuing internal rearrangements or external life changes the person has constructed based on insights gained in treatment?

In answering these questions, we ordinarily must depend on anecdotal reports from colleagues, our own personal experience, and hearing from the subset of patients who return for further consultation. In other words, we are at the mercy of serendipity in learning of subsequent growth or, on occasion, dismaying deterioration following a long therapy or analysis.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Postscript: Reconstructing Our Collective Past

Jennifer Stuart

Jennifer Stuart

11 Riverside Drive, 8FE
New York, NY 10023
E-mail: jstuart@rcn.com

Jennifer Stuart  

More than a century has passed since Freud first met Olga Hönig and Max Graf; nearly a century since he published the “Analysis of a Phobia in a Five-year-old Boy”; and a half-century since Herbert and Max Graf shared their recollections of “the Professor” with Kurt Eissler. What compels us to reexamine Freud's seminal case history so long after its conclusion, and what might we hope to gain from this exercise? How much can retrospection across a fifty-year gap tell us about the intentions and aims actually operating at the time of Hans's treatment? Do a few new morsels of information give us warrant to question Freud's original observations and conclusions? We weren't there! How does this inescapable fact motivate, limit, and perhaps enhance our understanding of what happened so long ago?

The passage of time and the deaths of the main players leave us wondering—with curiosity no longer fettered by deference to the living—what went on behind those closed doors. Like curious children, we set about our research. The same mixed motives—present, to varying degrees, in all of us—shape our individual responses to the material. Some, hoping for a glimpse of patrem nudam, seek snapshots of Freud in morally ambiguous poses (he checked into a hotel with Minna!). Others, hoping to uphold a cherished ideal, paint portraits of an unimpeachable forebear with a noble theoretical agenda, his errors few and largely inconsequential.

Each contributor to this section on Little Hans has tried to steer clear of both leering dismissal and blind idealization. This doesn't mean we've always succeeded, or that we can avoid other problems endemic to efforts at reconstruction. Like Hans (or any other child working at the mystery of his origins), we gather as much fact as possible, interpolating fantasy where fact fails. But we find that the line between fact and fantasy is a hard one to draw.

851

Putnam Camp: Sigmund Freud, James Jackson Putnam, and the Purpose of American Psychology. By George Prochnik. New York: Other Press, 2006, 455 pp., $29.95.

Sanford Gifford

Sanford Gifford

1 Hillside Place
Cambridge, MA 02140
Fax: 801-912-7445
E-mail: sgifford@hms.harvard.edu

Sanford Gifford   Associate Clinical Professor, Harvard Medical School; Director of Archives, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Chair, History and Archives Committee, American Psychoanalytic Association.

Over thirty years have elapsed since Nathan Hale's admirable biographical sketch of James Jackson Putnam, which introduced his publication of the Freud-Putnam correspondence (1971). Putnam, hailed by Freud as the first American to interest himself in psychoanalysis, served as the reluctant founder and president of the American Psychoanalytic Association in 1911. Lesser figures in the history of psychoanalysis have received lengthy biographies, and at last George Prochnik has filled the gap in full measure. He happens to be the ideal author for this task, as the great-grandson of Putnam himself, familiar with the New England traditions of the Putnam family camp from his own childhood, and the grandson of a Jewish physician who fled Vienna in 1938, with his memories of its well-upholstered haute bourgeoisie. Prochnik is also a professional journalist, with a witty and readable style and an omnivorous appetite for scholarly writings, family papers, and world literature.

Unlike Rosenzweig's detailed account (1992) of Putnam's meeting with Freud at the Clark Lectures in Worcester in 1909, Prochnik transports the reader directly from the lectures to the Putnam camp in the Adirondacks, where Freud, Jung, and Ferenczi spent three days “roughing” it. Family camps, or compounds, were a peculiarly American institution: country summer houses that varied from the opulent to very modest collections of cottages, usually centered around an isolated farm in the wilderness. Relatives, neighbors, and friends gathered there over many generations, and great-aunts and children of all ages were numerous. The Putnam camp, founded in 1885, was typical. with its emphasis on rustic living in the primeval forest, far from home.

I recommend that readers of this unusual collection of psychoanalytic papers start with chapter 12, where the unifying theme of the book appears in its clearest statement: “In my focus on the need to be comfortable with mystery, with poetry, with unknowing, I have attempted to highlight the different dimensions that psychoanalysts must bring to clinical work, if they are going to know who and where they are. Throughout the text I have alluded to the works of Meister Eckhart to suggest a broader reading of what it means to be human. None of this stands in opposition to analysts understanding the genetic determinants of a person's present situation, nor does it exclude a sensible analysis of defenses or transference manifestations” (p. 125).

[[RunningHead]]

III. Dimensions and New Measures Relevant to Psychoanalysis

The Creation of a Coding Scheme Assessing Curiosity Expression in Adolescent Interviews: Preliminary Findings

Ayelet Barkai and Stuart Hauser

Stuart Hauser

328 Broadway
Cambridge, MA 02139
E-mail: ayelet_barkai@hms.harvard.edu

Stuart Hauser  

Curiosity is conceptualized as adaptive and associated with positive psychosocial outcome (Kashdan, Rose, and Fincham 2004). Yet our knowledge of curiosity is surely limited. What are its developmental antecedents? How does curiosity function to promote cognitive, interand intrapersonal development? (Kashdan 2004). In constructing a new assessment of this vital capacity, our overarching aims are to elucidate(1) lines of curiosity development in lowand high-risk children and adolescents, and (2) the consequences of inhibited expression and development of curiosity.

Clinical research interview data from a 25-year longitudinal research project (Hauser, Powers, and Noam 1991; Hauser, Golden, and Allen 2006) are being used to assess and track adolescents' curiosity, a construct not yet studied in this data set. We propose three research questions: (1) Do specific facets of adolescent curiosity appear at different rates and in disparate forms? (2) Do pathways of curiosity development differ among same-age early adolescents? (3) How do various aspects of curiosity development predict academic success?

Interpersonal curiosity has been linked to positive relationship outcomes in young adults (Kashdan and Roberts 2004, 2006), as have the emergence of self-knowledge capacities (Bandura 1981; Parker 1997; Beardslee et al. 1990). However, we have discovered no studies investigating how curiosity may spark and facilitate the growth of selfknowledge and interpersonal relationships in adolescence and beyond.

287

Francis Baudry

1160 Fifth Avenue (111)

Francis Baudry   Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

In the discipline of psychoanalysis we try to anchor developing theories to clinical data or at least establish plausible connections between our theories and clinical material that can either confirm them or show them to be inconsistent. Of course we also know that the clinical data we select to demonstrate the usefulness of our theories is already dependent on some preconceived ideas about relevance. Hence our field is always struggling to define what constitutes validity and proof. As theories can never be “true” or “false,” they can only be judged by their usefulness.

These considerations are relevant to the critical evaluation of George Hagman's Aesthetic Experience, which presents a good overview of the field of aesthetics from the point of view of self psychology and relational psychoanalysis. Thorough and well researched, it brings together the ideas of Freud and of some of his followers, including contemporary theorists and developmental psychologists. Its chapter headings give a good perspective on its scope: The Developmental Matrix of Aesthetic Experience; Idealization; The Creative Process; The Sense of Beauty; Ugliness; The Sublime. The book also includes a brief philosophical review of the field of aesthetics before turning to more psychoanalytic considerations.

Hagman, an artist and poet, is an LCSW psychoanalyst on the faculty of the National Psychological Association for Psychoanalysis and of the Training and Research Institute for Self Psychology, both in New York.

I did not come across any clear references to his own experiences of creativity (painting or poetry), in spite of his allusions to it.

1431

The International Psychoanalysis Library, under the auspices of the International Psychoanalytical Association, offers in this volume the history of psychoanalysis in Latin America and the outstanding conceptual and technical contributions of the region to psychoanalytic ideas and practice. The IPA Publication Committee recognized that the important contributions of Latin America to psychoanalysis remain little known in other regions of the world. The committee selected Sergio Lewkowicz of Porto Alegre, Brazil, and Silvia Flechner of Montevideo, Uruguay, to approach the Board of Directors of FEPAL (Federación Psicoanalítica de América Latina) to propose a joint production of the book to be published simultaneously in English and in Spanish. They received an enthusiastic response due to the fact that FEPAL's members are well aware that analytic papers published by their component societies in local journals and books rarely reach analysts on other continents, because few analysts in other IPA regions read Spanish and in any case have limited access to the Latin American journals. Latin American economic restrictions make it practically impossible for local analysts to have their works translated into English, the prevalent language of the IPA, or into other European languages.

The editors discussed with the Publication Committee the psychoanalytic concepts that would represent original contributions by Latin American authors and after careful evaluation chose eight subjects and an historical introduction to be developed as chapters of the book. Finally, they selected prominent representatives of each conceptual approach to write the chapters. In an effort to widen the international dialogue, the editors invited a distinguished analyst from another part of the world to comment on each chapter.

III. Dimensions and New Measures Relevant to Psychoanalysis

Reliability of a Multidimensional Measure For Scoring Reflective Function

Lindsay Hill , Kenneth Levy , Kevin Meehan and Joseph Reynoso

Joseph Reynoso

240 Moore Building
University Park, PA 16803
Fax: 814-863-7002
E-mail: klevy@psu.edu

Joseph Reynoso  

How people understand their own minds and those of others has long been of interest to philosophers, psychoanalysts, neuroscientists, and theologians. Drawing on philosophy of mind and object relations and attachment theory, Peter Fonagy and colleagues (1998) coined the term reflective function (RF) to denote an individual's capacity to mentalize (or reflect upon) the thoughts, feelings, intentions, and desires of self and others. This capacity is developed in childhood and is dependent on the quality of interpersonal interactions with caregivers.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Endnote to Termination and Renaissance: Response to Commentaries

Joseph Caston

Joseph Caston

2710 Broderick Street
San Francisco, CA 94123
E-mail: joecaston@comcast.net

Joseph Caston  

Iam grateful for the thoughtfulness of Irene Willis's and Warren Poland's comments on my work on termination, and pleased that it has stimulated these interesting discussions. I appreciate where their positions align with mine, but will consider here challenges they point up or allude to. I also want to reflect on Poland's ideas regarding the potential of aesthetics to illuminate or deepen analytic thinking.

To begin: when a long-term open-ended treatment enters a naturally terminating process, it is for a time difficult for either patient or doctor to notice or bear it. Wanting to think more about this, I sought clues from how we understand or have expectations about stories, whether lived or imagined, as in treatment, poetry, or other narrations. I broadly assume that the matrix out of which literary and clinical crafts develop engenders shared features, just because each works as a spoken enterprise between two minds, and also, as Poland has put it well, each moves to capture some essence of persons, beyond the uttered words. Thus we may apply elements from either genre to illuminate the other.

When Willis lets us in on her experiences with crafting the endings of poems, we may be able to relate what we discover there to the problem of how others approach the reading of a therapy's ending. She discloses that when she anticipates that there can be good finishing lines to a poem in process, she feels a metaphorical petit mort—presumably a kick of real satisfaction. She also indicates that there can be a “click at the end, like the lid of a box snapping shut [that] tells me the poem is there….”1 We grant that any such creation is forever subject to further revision and so, potentially never finished—yet how well a boxlid snapped shut expresses the sense of an ending! If this effect works

1The intuitive notion of a “click,” by the way, also comes up in classical psychoanalytic practice (Fenichel 1941; Greenacre 1968; Waelder in Guttman 1987). It applies to incisive moments, first in the analyst's mind, and then in the patient's, when an effective interpretation is received. For Fenichel (p. 8) “clicking” was criterial and meant that the analytic couple is “working on the level of psychic reality,” that “one feels with the patient's ‘genuine’ utterances and the analyst's correct interpretations.” “Genuine” here means unfiltered by one's defensiveness. But what this click really is and what goes into it—e.g., internal matchups, resonances, and puzzle plug-ins—await a fuller and (I believe) accessible elucidation.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Attachment Theory and Cognitive Science: Commentary on Fonagy and Target

Philip Robbins and Jeffrey Zacks

Jeffrey Zacks

Campus Box 1073
St. Louis, MO 63130-4899
Fax: 314-935-7349
E-mail: probbins@artsci.wustl.edu

Campus Box 1125
St. Louis, MO 63130-4899
Fax: 314-935-7588
E-mail: jzacks@artsci.wustl.edu

Jeffrey Zacks   Associate Professor of Psychology, Washington University, St. Louis.

Peter Fonagy and Mary Target present a broad and provocative argument for the potential contributions of attachment theory to clinical psychoanalysis, together with an analysis of why these contributions have thus far not been realized. Central to the authors” discussion is the claim that the movement within contemporary cognitive science known as “embodied cognition” provides the theoretical machinery to bring attachment theory and psychoanalysis together. This conclusion depends on two claims about attachment theory's relationship to cognitive science. First, Fonagy and Target argue that attachment theory as originally developed by Bowlby was based on a concept of mental representation derived from classical, “disembodied” cognitive science. Second, they argue that what is new about contemporary cognitive science is its turn to embodied cognition. These claims support their conclusion that attachment theory should be revised by bringing it more into line with contemporary cognitive science.

Our perspective is that of working cognitive scientists—one of us is a philosopher of mind and the other a cognitive neuroscientist. From our perspective, the conceptual map presented by Fonagy and Target has recognizable landmarks, but deviates from our experience of the terrain.

This commentary was a fully collaborative effort. The order of the authors” names is arbitrary.

457

[[RunningHead]]

Emptiness in Agoraphobic Patients: Commentary on Milrod

Peter Dunn

Peter Dunn

308 East 73rd Street
New York, NY 10021
Fax: 212-754-2690
E-mail: pbdunnmd@aol.com

Peter Dunn  

The fundamental problem in agoraphobia, Barbara Milrod argues, is a developmental arrest. Agoraphobics view themselves as weak and incomplete. In order to sustain a feeling of safety they are compelled to live out the fantasy that they are fused with a powerful and loving superperson. They remain anxious nonetheless because this fantasy is always on the verge of being toppled by the reality of the phobic companion's limitations and by their own basic ambivalence. Defenses against aggression are only partly successful, so inevitably agoraphobics make their phobic companion suffer.

Milrod's theory departs from the classical model, but her views express the current clinical consensus. In particular, her emphasis on the agoraphobic's broad-scale character pathology is consistent with the widespread clinical conviction that at this point in history agoraphobic patients from the industrialized Western world suffer invariably from severe character pathology. Milrod's emphasis on the central dynamic role of preoedipal ambivalence is also consistent with the substantial trend in the literature since the mid 1970s. While this may simply reflect the switch away from the once dominant oedipal paradigm, most analysts would agree with Milrod that the shift is consistent with increased clinical sophistication about agoraphobia and greater theoretical clarity about the nature of oedipal/preoedipal condensations.

Scattered through this paper are three complementary explanations as to why agoraphobics feel empty. The first is that agoraphobics complain of feeling empty because they are empty.

Director of Clinical Services, New York Psychoanalytic Institute and Society.

1027

[[RunningHead]]

Embodiment and our Immersion with Others: Commentary on Fonagy and Target

Robert Emde

Robert Emde

Box C268-69
4200 East Ninth Avenue
Denver, CO 80262
E-mail: bob.emde@uchsc.edu

Robert Emde  

eading Fonagy and Target's essay is a pleasure. In critical scientific discourse it is all too typical for authors to dissect differences in points of view between traditions or within traditions over time; this then leads to an argument for a new speculative view that tries to take into account current knowledge but that in many respects leaves the reader hanging. Fonagy and Target do much more. They review cogently, and brilliantly, the early history of Bowlby's attachment theory and its shunning by psychoanalysis, its subsequent modification through research using observation and experiment, its “move to representations,” and its recent acceptance into psychoanalytic thinking, particularly with the ascendancy of relational psychoanalytic views. They also point to the fact that Bowlby's thinking on the cognitive aspects of attachment theory, though strongly multidisciplinary, was in large measure based on an information-processing model in the cognitive sciences, a model that has been superseded in the newer cognitive neuro sciences, in which links between mental functioning and brain/body are being discovered. In the midst of newly emergent technology and methods, mental functioning is seen not as abstracted (as in the former computer metaphor that guided information processing) but as “embodied” such that plausible neurobiological events are sought to account for mental events. Surprisingly, the authors point out, not only does this new research-based model put a different light on some of attachment theory, but it also draws forward some historical elements of psychoanalytic theory that in our times have been relatively neglected.

Professor of Psychiatry, Emeritus, University of Colorado Health Sciences Center; faculty, Denver Institute for Psychoanalysis.

485

[[RunningHead]]

Emptiness in Agoraphobia Patients

Barbara Milrod

Barbara Milrod

525 East 68th Street
Payne Whitney Clinic
New York, NY 10024-3401
Fax: 212-746-5944
E-mail: bmilrod@mail.med.cornell.edu

Barbara Milrod  

In light of new research findings about the efficacy of psychodynamic treatment for panic disorder and agoraphobia, it seems a prudent time to carefully address psychoanalytic thinking about the treatment of agoraphobia. The literature has highlighted oedipal contributions to its genesis and clinical unraveling in psychoanalysis. While those contributions are indeed central to the disorder, structural deficits in the self-representation often become a central focus of treatment once symptomatic remission has been achieved in psychoanalytic treatment. This aspect of the clinical presentation of agoraphobia has not yet been specifically addressed in the psychiatric literature. Some aspects of the phenomenon have been described by psychoanalysts. It is more difficult to treat this “emptiness” than the overt symptoms of agoraphobia, as described in DSM-IV. Nonetheless, this phenomenon may be one of the contributors to the chronicity of the disorder. Two clinical cases illustrate these points.

Play in the Psychoanalytic Situation

Frances Lang

Frances Lang

85 Kenwood Street
Brookline, MA 02446-2412
E-mail: lang.f@rcn.com

Frances Lang  

This stimulating panel discussion promoted thinking about adult, as well as child, analysis. The tasks of acknowledging affect and of distinguishing between fantasy and external reality, aspects of treatment addressed by the panelists, confront adult and child populations with equal poignancy.

Karen Gilmore introduced the panel by highlighting general issues regarding play in the session and focusing on play as central in the analysis of prepubertal children. Increasingly, she stated, play has come to be understood as serving a crucial role in child development: “The ability to play is viewed as a developmentally determined capacity with strong biological foundations serving a range of social, interpersonal, and intrapsychic functions, and observable in the young of all cultures, mammals and birds.” From a psychoanalytic perspective, play evolves with cognitive and psychological development and is growthpromoting despite its potential to be recruited to conflict.

In the evaluative phase of treatment, playing with the child serves as a diagnostic tool for assessing trauma, level of ego organization, and degree of constriction, disorganization, or impoverishment of inner life. The individual's psychological and cognitive development and degree of interpersonal relatedness are revealed through play; similarly, shifts in play can provide evidence of progress in the course of treatment.

Throughout a child analysis, play remains a primary means of communication, revealing the child's coping strategies and, especially, serving as the primary medium for the playing out of interactions among the child's unconscious, the object world, and the transference relationship. Whatever problems may deform the child's play function, the child analyst anticipates that most children “will produce some form of play that can serve as a shared region … where the action of the

Panel held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Washington DC, June 17, 2006.

[[RunningHead]]

Poster Summaries II. Human Development: Intersections with Psychoanalytic Perspectives

Maternal and Fetal Representations, Dimensions of Personality, and Prenatal Attachment in Women Hospitalized with High-Risk Pregnancy

Anna Brandon , Sandra Pitts , Richard Robinson and C. Stringer

C. Stringer

5323 Harry Hines Boulevard
UT Southwestern Medical Center at Dallas
Dallas, TX 75235
E-mail: anna.brandon@utsouthwestern.edu

Dallas

C. Stringer  

The purpose of this study is to investigate the influence of an expectant mother's personality style, maternal object representations, and ability to develop an internal representation of her fetus upon the quality and intensity of maternal fetal attachment. The exploration of the power of object representations and personality to predict prenatal attachment (also referred to in the literature as antenatal attachment) in the context of hospitalization during high-risk pregnancy would have relevance in both intervention and standard of care for such mothers. Since this is a highly specialized sample of individuals, it is expected that other variables may have predictive value. Earlier depressive episodes, current levels of depression, severity of maternal/fetal risk, and gestational age of the fetus at the onset of complications will be taken into account.

Background

Alexandra Harrison and Edward Tronick

Edward Tronick

183 Brattle Street
Cambridge, MA 02138

Edward Tronick  

We are grateful to Judith Chused for her close reading of our paper and for her intelligent and thoughtful comments. Although we agree with her in the main, her review suggests the need for us to clarify two points.

The first is in response to Chused's “disagreement” with us about whether adults and adolescents communicate primarily in abstract language, in contrast to young children and infants, who use more nonverbal communication. Chused states, “Although there is much ‘talk' in the analyses of older individuals, much [meaning] is also communicated implicitly, nonverbally, in all analyses.” We agree with Chused that adolescents and adults also communicate nonverbally in important ways. Our intention was not to minimize the nonverbal communication of adults, but rather to emphasize the fact that no matter what the mode of communication—language, gestures, postural change—communication takes place and meaning gets made. We also wished to elaborate the idea of “age-possible” meaning-making, a concept that distinguishes the dyadic expansion model from other models of change derived from infant research.

Adolescents and adults of course do communicate nonverbally. They also communicate using language and abstract thought, and the presence of these additional capacities changes their entire way of making meaning, even when they are using their bodies to communicate in nonverbal ways. This change arises because when they choose (consciously or nonconsciously) to communicate nonverbally, adolescents and adults are communicating in the context of an expanded repertoire, one that includes the capacity for verbal communication. By contrast, in the case of an infant or a young child with minimal capacity for language or abstract thought, the repertoire of meaning-making capacities is more limited, and it follows that the meaning of the nonverbal communication is different. Thus, meaning is age-possible (specific to a developmental level).

Paul Schwaber   Professor of Letters, Wesleyan University; faculty, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis.

Hamlet draws us into its rendered world, enabling us to experience it with depth, awareness, and resonance, in a mode we recognize as aesthetic. By way of Shakespeare's play—primarily the first act—and a detailed case study, aesthetic and psychoanalytic experience are compared, to suggest that, for our own analytic discourse, we revalue Freud's unease that his case studies read like short stories.

On the cold battlements of Elsinore, a soldier, coming to the relief of another on watch, asks edgily into the dark: “Who's there?” Grateful to meet, they are joined by a third and by Horatio, a close friend of Hamlet's. Together they talk uneasily about something eerie. For two nights, the late King Hamlet's ghost has appeared, arrayed in full armor but saying nothing. Horatio, university-educated and skeptical, has come expecting to distinguish between what must be their fearful fantasies and actuality. When lo, the ghost appears again: solemn, impressive, and undeniable. Horatio coaxes and pleads with it to speak, to announce its purpose, but it will not. The group speculate that armed thus for battle, the ghost may fear that young Fortinbras of Norway aims to recapture land King Hamlet had wrested from the Norwegian's father. The king's ghost—we will eventually learn—will speak only to his son, to reveal that he has been murdered by his brother Claudius and that it is Hamlet's task—if ever he his dear father loved— to take revenge. Claudius also has seduced the queen (whether before

Plenary address, American Psychoanalytic Association Washington, DC, June 16, 2006. Esman, M.D. lecture) the Richardson History of Psychiatry Seminar, Weill Cornell Medical College. Professor Tom Huhn, School of Visual Arts, provided a particularly helpful commentary. Submitted for publication September 25, 2006.

Since his death a quarter of a century ago, Jacques Lacan's ideas have not gone away (as many wished), nor have they quietly infiltrated mainstream psychoanalysis (as many feared). His work is read and cited more and more, many institutes offer their advanced candidates an elective on Lacan, and the number of books in English attempting to convey his ideas are too numerous to list. Many are still being written in the kind of idealizing jargon that serves primarily to sustain a cult and leaves readers feeling irritated and excluded. The posture of an “official” dogma with its correct interpretation, which was so offensive in the past (Muller 1988), has become less overt and the reader will not find it in the books reviewed here.

The authors of History beyond Trauma have distilled in this volume thirty years of psychoanalytic work with patients who suffer intermittent psychotic breakdown. Although they have consulted with patients in hospital settings, the bulk of their work in Paris is with individual patients seen in their private offices. To provide containment, they rely not on external constraints or medication but rather on the recognition provided by the symbolic exchanges at work in the analytic relationship.

Their thesis is original but straightforward: the chief cause of madness (their word for psychosis)

Beneath the Surface of the Therapeutic Interaction: The Psychoanalytic Method in Modern Dress

Wilma Bucci and Bernard Maskit

Bernard Maskit

Garden City, NY 11530
E-mail: bucci@panther.adelphi.edu

Stony Brook, NY 11794-3651
E-mail: bernie@math.sunysb.edu
E-mail: daap@optonline.net

Bernard Maskit  

This study represents a new generation of psychotherapy process research, using multiple perspectives on the data of the analytic situation, including impressions of the treating analyst, ratings of complete sessions by clinical judges, and objective linguistic measures. Computerized measures of language style developed in the framework of multiple code theory were applied to verbatim session recordings from a psychoanalytic case; the measures are illustrated in microanalyses of the process in two sessions. The results show agreement between the linguistic measures and clinical ratings based on a psychoanalytic perspective. The linguistic measures look beneath the surface of the therapeutic interaction by relying largely on lexical items of which clinicians are not likely to be explicitly aware, and enable a new perspective on the therapeutic discourse as seen in the graphic images of the microprocess. While the results of this study were limited to a single case, the automatized measures can be readily applied to large samples and in repeated single case designs. Two goals of process research, using measures such as those developed in this study, are discussed: to develop measures of mediating variables that can be used to identify specific treatment effects in comparative outcome studies; and, beyond this pragmatic aim, to assess development of capacities for self-exploration and self-regulation as psychoanalytic treatment goals.

[[RunningHead]]

The Dynamics of Empirically Derived Factors in the Therapeutic Relationship

Eric Nuetzel , Randy Larsen and Zvjezdana Prizmic

Zvjezdana Prizmic

8820 Ladue Road, Suite 301
St. Louis, MO 63124
Fax: 314-361-6269
E-mail: ejnuetz@swbell.net

Zvjezdana Prizmic  

The therapeutic relationship is the source of major concepts in psychoanalytic clinical theory. Such concepts as resistance, transference, countertransference, and the alliance are fundamental, even though there may be shifts in meaning between theoretical schools and clinical contexts. In the clinical psychoanalytic literature, disagreement exists over the nature of the alliance and its essential components. Empirical studies using reliable patient, therapist, and observer scales to assess the alliance demonstrate a correlation with psychotherapeutic gains. In the study reported here, thirteen patients were followed for 6 to 33 months of psychodynamic psychotherapy, during which time their views of the therapeutic relationship were assessed, and several experiential measures taken, all on a weekly basis. Statistical analyses reveal that the therapeutic relationship, as reflected in the patients' weekly responses to the St. Louis Therapeutic Relationship Rating Scale, has four distinct components: therapeutic alliance, resistance, transference love, and negative transference. On a week-by-week basis, the therapeutic alliance was the strongest predictor of improvement in patient-reported general adjustment, as reflected in such areas as self-esteem, positive affect, social relations, work productivity, satisfaction, and optimism. Time plots of the variables show the typical time course for the components of the therapeutic relationship, as well as for improvement on the experiential variables. Results indicate that the therapeutic alliance, transference, and resistance are central components of the psychotherapeutic relationship, which in turn predict the ongoing life experience of the patient.

Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis: Fifty Years Later

Kathryn Zerbe

Kathryn Zerbe

3181 SW Sam Jackson Park Road

Kathryn Zerbe  

Richard Fox began by reviewing the historical antecedents in American psychoanalysis regarding the differences and similarities between psychoanalysis and psychotherapy. He noted that a panel on the same subject was held fifty years ago at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association (Rangell 1954a; Freud 1954; Stone 1954). A key question centered upon the defining elements of psychoanalysis.

The panelists, who included Leo Stone, Edith Jacobson. and Anna Freud, agreed that a psychoanalytic process is defined by structural change occurring after the development of a transference neurosis. They agreed also that resolution of the transference neurosis by interpretation alone is the primary contributant to therapeutic change in analysis. Because psychotherapy does not entail the resolution of a transference neurosis, its benefits were deemed more modest than those of a full psychoanalytic procedure.

Other elements that that panel viewed as definitive of psychoanalysis included significant regression and the absence of parameters. When parameters had to be employed, it was thought, they should be thoroughly analyzed by the end of the analysis. Patients in psychotherapy were believed to benefit from identification with the therapist, not from interpretation and working through of the transference. Fox underscored that in the 1950s psychoanalysis was deemed the “gold standard of treatment” by most analysts, who relegated psychotherapy to a secondary, inferior role.

Panel held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Seattle, June 10, 2005.

Panelists: Richard Fox (chair), Rosemary Balsam, Arnold Rothstein, Kenneth Eisold.

[[RunningHead]]

What if There is an Ethical Dimension in Psychoanalysis?

W. Meissner S.J.

W. Meissner

Boston College
St. Mary's Hall
Chestnut Hill, MA 02467-3806
E-mail: meissner@bc.edu

W. Meissner  

The implications of an ethical dimension in psychoanalysis are explored, together with the questions whether and in what sense analysis has anything to contribute to ethical reflection, and whether and in what sense ethical considerations can play a role in analytic theory and process. As regards theory, analytic construction of an ethical agent would require a refocusing of structural and dynamic considerations in order to provide an integrative source of decision-making capacity to serve as the responsible agent in ethical deliberation, choice, and action. Suggestions are made for formulating the nature of the ethical agent in terms of a theory of motivation rather than of drive, and in terms of an integrative theory of the self. To the extent that analysis involves ethical considerations, the way lies open for dialogue and mutually enriching contributions of analysis to ethics and vice versa. Various aspects of the analytic perspective are explored that in themselves carry ethical implications or can be enriched by ethical reflection. Beyond theoretical considerations, the interaction between ethical factors and genetic, structural, and dynamic issues in clinical psychoanalysis loom as matters for future exploration.

[[RunningHead]]

Cannibalism and the Work of Culture in Bereavement: Commentary on Gottlieb

Beth Conklin

Beth Conklin

Nashville, TN 37235
E-mail: beth.a.conklin@vanderbilt.edu

Beth Conklin  

What thou lovest well remains,

the rest is dross

What thou lovest well shall not be

reft from thee

—Ezra Pound, “Canto 81” (the final text in Sally Mann's What Remains)

Visions of fragmented and reunified human bodies are prime imagery through which people experience loss and isolation and project desires for reparation. Richard Gottlieb's insightful essay shows how concerns with bodily integrity, including cannibalistic imagery, come to the fore in diverse settings, suggesting this is powerful imaginative material for confronting existential dilemmas and emotions that are among the most universal human experiences.

As an ethnographer who works with indigenous people for whom cannibalism used to be an integral part of their cultural framework for managing death and mourning, I find the connections Gottlieb identifies exciting and thought-provoking. The Wari' of western Brazil explain their former practice of consuming the dead in terms of an indigenous theory of memory and grief that emphasizes the body's role in relationships and the effect of visual imagery in shaping emotional responses. Gottlieb's model helps make sense of this and other nonWestern practices of bodily destruction, especially in contexts of bereavement and mourning.

Associate Professor of Anthropology and Religious Studies, Vanderbilt University.

Submitted for publication June 26, 2006.

Barry Landau's introduction emphasized the panelists' wish to engage the audience in a discussion of clinical material presented by Steven Ablon and James Herzog. Each of these child analysts would present a detailed account of his work with a child with a severe form of biologically and/or developmentally based psychopathology. The work would then be discussed by Peter Fonagy. Landau anticipated that the panelists' presentations would raise important questions about psychoanalytic techniques, what happens when these techniques are applied to children with severe forms of psychopathology, and the nature of these disorders, including the degree to which they are amenable to psychoanalysis and psychoanalytically oriented modes of treatment.

Ablon presented material from the analysis of Josh, an elevenyear-old boy tormented by terrifying, chaotic impulses that seemed to emerge from raw, unfiltered sexual and aggressive images. Within days of his initial consultation, and again six months later, hospitalization was required. In addition to four-times-weekly psychoanalysis, Josh's treatment plan included medication, regular monthly meetings with the parents, and (at the parents' request) once-weekly cognitivebehavioral therapy.

Throughout the first year of his analysis, Josh battled an overwhelming level of anxiety. At times he seemed to be struggling to maintain his sanity.

Panel held at the Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 22, 2005.

Panelists: Barry J. Landau (chair), Steven L. Ablon, James M. Herzog. Discussant: Peter Fonagy.

923

The Second Person

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

180 North Michigan Avenue #2220
Chicago, IL 60601
E-mail: belitowitz@aol.com

Bonnie Litowitz  

Unlike third-person sciences, psychoanalysis is the science of the second person. Briefly tracing the history of our focus on a second person, this paper contrasts two different approaches—the dyadic and the dialogic, proposing the latter as the better model for our field and the one that marks our unique contribution to other disciplines.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Individual Differences in Melancholy Gender Among Women: Does Ambivalence Matter?

Meg Jay

Meg Jay

3036 Regent Street
Berkeley, CA 94705
E-mail: megjay@berkeley.edu

Meg Jay  

This research offers an empirical investigation inspired by Butler's theory of melancholy gender (1995) and a revision of this theory (Jay 2007a). Psychoanalytic feminist theory is drawn on to suggest that melancholy and gender are more likely to be associated in female development than in male development, and Freud's theory of melancholy (1917) is taken to suggest that ambivalence predicts individual differences in melancholy gender among women. In a longitudinal study of women's adult development, an examination of femininity, depressive symptoms, and ambivalence in attachment was conducted in order to evaluate these claims. Findings show that depressive symptoms and femininity are significantly correlated within the sample, but that individual differences in melancholy gender exist. To understand these differences, an analysis was conducted to determine whether ambivalence in attachment accounts for the relation between depressive symptoms and femininity; complementary analyses examined whether low ambivalence in attachment attenuates, or lessens, the relation between femininity and depressive symptoms. Results from these analyses support the notion that it is not the loss and internalization of the same-sex object choice per se that results in melancholy gender in women, as Butler argues; rather, it is the internalization of a lost, ambivalent same-sex attachment that forges the link between melancholy and gender. Narrative material is presented to personify melancholy and unmelancholy gender.

Contributions to Understanding Therapeutic Change: Now We have a Playground

Alexandra Harrison and Edward Tronick

Edward Tronick

183 Brattle Street
Cambridge, MA 02138
E-mail: aharrisonmd@comcast.net

1295 Boylston Street, Suite 320
Boston, MA 02115
Fax: 617-730-0074
E-mail: ed.tronick@childrens.harvard.edu

Edward Tronick  

The understanding of therapeutic change is explored in two ways. The first is by providing a model of change that emphasizes moment-to-moment, “local-level” interactions in the analytic dyad. The second is to offer detailed clinical information—taken from the videotape of a child analyst's first session with a three-year-old girl—that illustrates how this change model can be useful to clinicians. The clinical material is presented in the form of verbal trascripts and descriptions of nonverbal communicative exchanges between child and analyst. Both the model of change and the technique of videotape microanalysis build upon work by infant researchers and thus show how advances in related fields can supplement traditional psychoanalytic methods.

[[RunningHead]]

Poster Summaries II. Human Development: Intersections with Psychoanalytic Perspectives

It's Hard to Say: The Challenge of Connecting Emotions and Language for First-Time Mothers

Gregory Lowder , Wilma Bucci , Bernard Maskit and Christopher Christian

Christopher Christian

103 East 86th Street
Apartment #12C
New York, NY 10028
E-mail: greglowder@aol.com

103 East 86th Street
Apartment #12C
NewYork, NY 10028

Christopher Christian  

This research is driven by the question, How do people connect emotions and language? More specifically, How do people communicate their subjective experience of emotions verbally and nonverbally in a manner that creates personal relevance for the speaker, and allows others to vicariously experience such emotion?

Bucci (1997) postulates that people use three different systems to represent and process information, including emotional information. These three systems, the subsymbolic, the nonverbal symbolic, and the verbal, constitute her Multiple Code structure. The concept of the Referential Process is at the heart of this theory, as it provides a model of how people connect subsymbolic experience to verbal symbols. The Referential Process can be measured by the Discourse Attributes Analysis Program (DAAP) text analysis system (Maskit and Bucci 2005), which provides output for multiple dictionaries.

Bucci's work addresses the challenging question of how talk therapy works, but it has wider implications as well.

Überleben und Versuche der Wiederbelebung. By Hillel Klein. Edited by Christoph Biermann and Carl Nedelmann. Translated by Barbara Strehlow. Stuttgart: Frommann Holzboog, 2003, 319 pp., €52.00.

Marion Oliner

Marion Oliner

670 West End Avenue # 9F
New York, NY 10025
E-mail: marionoliner@earthlink.net

Marion Oliner   New York Freudian Society, NPAP, International Psychoanalytical Association.

Hillel Klein, an analyst who survived life in the ghetto and in concentration camps and who died in 1985 at the age of sixty-two, wrote and published many important papers. At the time of his death he was preparing this book, Überleben und Versuche der Wiederbelebung, which two German editors, Christoph Biermann and Carl Nedelmann,

341

have only recently brought out.1 The work is remarkable because of Klein's understanding of the circumstances of the lives of the survivors, before, during, and after the attempt by the Nazis to exterminate the Jews. Giving the individuals he describes a label, however, runs counter to the spirit of Klein's writings. Nowhere in these pages does one have the impression that he is describing a group whose characteristics can be grasped in general terms, and unlike most of the literature on trauma, Klein does not focus on the deficits without bringing out the characteristics that enabled Jews to survive and to create new lives for themselves and their descendants.

A large part of the work approaches the trauma of the Holocaust from the perspective of those who reintegrated themselves into the various groups among whom they lived after the war. Not that the book is silent on the trauma of the extermination of millions of Jews. The editors selected a number of essays, about half of the book, devoted to the description of the various conditions under which Jews lived during the persecutions, such as life in the ghetto, in hiding, and in the camps themselves. From these pages it is clear that the author either had firsthand experience with these conditions and the emotional consequences of living under these circumstances, or he treated survivors after the war who familiarized him with conditions he had not known personally. Further, he examines the impact of the postwar silence about their experiences, forced upon the survivors for internal as well as external reasons, which varied according to the individuals and according to the culture into which the survivors integrated themselves. Thus, the mission animating the survival of many—to bear witness and thereby keeping the memory of the dead alive—could not be accomplished for many years. In Israel, the Eichmann trial eventually opened this history to public knowledge.

To Redeem One Person Isto Redeem the World: The Life of Frieda Fromm-Reichmann. By Gail A. Hornstein. New York: Other Press, 2005, 512 pp., $22.00.

Barbara Almond

Barbara Almond

550 Hamilton Avenue, Suite 202
Palo Alto, CA 94301
E-mail: brmalmond@gmail.com

Barbara Almond   Training and Supervising Analyst, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute; Assistant Clinical Professor, Emerita, Stanford University Medical Center.

It took Gail Hornstein, a professor of psychology at Mount Holyoke College, ten years of hard work to produce this biography of Frieda Fromm-Reichmann, the only one in existence. When Hornstein began her researches, she intended to write a history of the psychotherapeutic treatment of schizophrenia. She soon found that this subject was inextricably connected to the life of its perhaps most famous advocate and practitioner. The author has done a yeoman's job in piecing together Fromm-Reichmann's life and work. Very little is known of her early life in Germany, and all of her personal papers have remained inaccessible; yet Hornstein did have access to “an extraordinarily rich archive of her clinical work, including dozens of tape recordings and verbatim transcripts of her therapy hours with schizophrenic patients,” which “offer a rare glimpse of an artful therapist hard at work” (p. xxv). Hornstein obtained, as well, hundreds of interviews with people who knew, or had been treated by, Fromm-Reichmann. This lucid and well-written book is at one and the same time a biography, an insightful psychological profile of its subject, and a very inclusive history of the complex (and often bitter) psychiatric and psychoanalytic controversies surrounding the treatment of severe mental disorders in the twentieth century.

In the strongest part of this biography, Hornstein carefully elaborates the development of Fromm-Reichmann's ideas and methods of treatment with psychotic patients at Chestnut Lodge, the unique mental hospital with which she was associated from the mid 1930s (after escaping to the United States from Nazi Germany) to her death in 1957. Further, the author includes a very intelligent discussion of the impact of I Never Promised You a Rose Garden (Greenberg 1964)

Truth, Growth, and Deception: The Late Seminars of Wilfred Bion

The Italian Seminars. By Wilfred R. Bion. London: Karnac Books, 2005, 109 pp., $23.95.The Tavistock Seminars. By Wilfred R Bion. London: Karnac Books, 2005, 118 pp., $23.95.

Howard Levine

Howard Levine

124 Dean Road
Brookline, MA 02445
E-mail: hblevine@aol.com

Howard Levine   Faculty, Psychoanalytic Institute of New England, East; faculty and Supervising Analyst, Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis.

For many American analysts, the work of Wilfred Bion is an acquired taste. His papers (Bion 1967) and early books are built on a foundation of Kleinian theory that may be unfamiliar to an audience raised on ego psychology and contemporary conflict theory. Attention and Interpretation (Bion 1970) and the various “lectures” and “seminars” that follow, can present even more of a problem. They can require familiarity—or at least comfort and conversance—with concepts such as “alpha and beta elements,” “container and contained,” “waking dream thoughts,” “thoughts without a thinker,” and Bion's infamous Grid. The result may seem baffling and esoteric, almost cultic. Is Bion writing as analyst, existentialist, philosopher, or mystic? What is the clinical relevance of his ideas? Will the effort required to master a new technical vocabulary prove worthwhile?

It is our great fortune that these two books, assembled and edited by Bion's widow, Francesca, from transcriptions of seminars given in the last years of his life, offer readers not only the opportunity to answer these questions for themselves, but a jargon-free entrèe into the mind of a consummate psychoanalyst and a brilliant thinker. Like Descartes, Bion is in search of an irreducible starting point from which to examine our knowledge of the mind and of the world: a point of certainty, analogous to the Cartesian cogito, from which all else can follow.

677

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

III. Dimensions and New Measures Relevant to Psychoanalysis

Psychometric Properties of a Chinese Version of the Psychological Mindedness Scale

Wang Hua , Mark Beitel , Zev Schuman-Olivier and Declan Barry

Declan Barry

China

495 Congress Avenue, 2nd Floor
New Haven, CT 06519
Fax: 203-781-4606
E-mail: beitelmark@aol.com

Declan Barry  

Psychological Mindedness (PM) is a construct originally conceptualized by psychodynamically oriented psychotherapists to describe the psychological quality of perceiving relationships among thoughts, feelings, and actions, and thus appreciating their influence on human behavior (Appelbaum 1973; Beitel et al. 2005). In addition, high PM has been thought to predict a better response to treatment in psychotherapy patients (Conte, Ratto, and Karasu 1996; Farber 1985, 1989), with increased retention and engagement in the therapeutic process (McCallum and Piper 1990).

Recent studies have examined relationships among PM and hypothetically related concepts, such as personality and attachment security (Beitel and Cecero 2003). Openness to Experience ([.beta] = 0.33, t = 5.22, p < .01) and Extraversion ([.beta] = 0.21, t = 2.96, p < .01), on the NEO-Five Factor Inventory (NEO-FFI) were positively associated with PM, while an inverse association was found with Neuroticism ([.beta] = -0.19,t = -2.70, p < .01). Attachment to Peers ([.beta] = 0.15, t = 2.14,p < .05), on the Inventory of Parent and Peer Attachment (IPPA) was significantly associated with PM: Attachment to Mother and Attachment to Father were not significantly related to PM.

Psychological Mindedness and Ethnic Differences

Ethnic differences have not emerged in recent studies of PM (Beitel and Cecero 2003; Beitel, Ferrer, and Cecero 2004, 2005). However, the number of Asian Americans in any given sample has been low. We have recently begun to develop norms for the PM Scale and have developed a database of 750 college students. Our recent analysis (Beitel et al. 2007) of this large data set revealed that Asian Americans (n = 61, M = 131.78, SD= 14.01) reported statistically significantly lower PM Scale scores (ANOVA,F = 4.00, p = .008) than blacks (n = 61, M = 133.67, SD = 11.75), Hispanics (n = 88, M = 134.69, SD = 11.87), and whites (n = 458, M = 136.80, SD = 12.27).

[[RunningHead]]

III. Dimensions and New Measures Relevant to Psychoanalysis

Differential Selectivity of Patients Assigned to Long-Term Psychoanalytic Treatment

Caspar Berghout and Jolien Zevalkink

Jolien Zevalkink

Olympiaplein 4
1076 AB Amsterdam
Netherlands
E-mail: berghout@npsai.nl

Jolien Zevalkink  

From 1945 onward, patients have found their way to long-term ambulatory psychoanalytic treatment (psychoanalytic psychotherapy and psychoanalysis) in the government-funded Netherlands mental health care system. So far it is not entirely clear what differences exist between patients assigned to either psychoanalytic psychotherapy (PP) or psychoanalysis (PA). Both clinicians and researchers indicate a clear need for systematic empirical data on differential selectivity for patients assigned to psychoanalytic treatment (Gunderson and Gabbard 1999). In the Netherlands, a monitoring system was developed to register patients' mental health status before and during treatment in order to systematically gather empirical data of this sort.

Psychosis and Near Psychosis: Ego Function, Symbol Structure, Treatment. By Eric R. Marcus Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 2003, xix + 412 pp., €49.95.M

Alan Sugarman

Alan Sugarman

4180 La Jolla Village Drive, Suite 550B
La Jolla, CA 92037
E-mail: mumford2@cox.net

Alan Sugarman   Training and Supervising Analyst and Supervising Child and Adolescent Analyst, San Diego Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of California at San Diego.

Like the first edition of Eric Marcus's Psychosis and Near Psychosis, this revised second edition makes important theoretical and clinical contributions that risk being lost because of unfortunate problems in how they are presented. One of the book's strengths, but also one of its weaknesses, is the complexity of thought that has gone into it. This

705

complexity is impossible to do justice to in a brief review; unfortunately, it is also likely to frighten away all but those who are particularly interested in the subject matter or intrepidly desirous of understanding the many applications of psychoanalytic thought. The book is essentially an attempt to integrate psychoanalytic understanding with cognitive neuroscience and psychopharmacology in order to apply that integration to general psychiatric notions about psychotic and borderline conditions.

Such an ambitious agenda, not surprisingly, can be at once both laudable and problematic. For example, many (though not all) practicing psychoanalysts would agree with Marcus's thesis that nonbiological etiological models of psychosis have proven problematic. But his efforts to forge an integration between biological and psychological factors can at times be off-putting to nonmedical analysts. In his introduction he notes the failure of general psychiatry to consider psycho-dynamic factors and emphasizes how his integration “makes the general psychiatric categories of illness richer and more understandable to the treating physician” p. (xii). It seems unnecessary and surprisingly out of date in the current psychoanalytic era to cede psychopathology to the discipline of psychiatry or to assume that treating analysts will be physicians. Such an assumption risks antagonizing nonmedical analysts, who have seen the equation of psychoanalyst with physician disappear in our professional literature over the past forty years. There are many nonmedical analysts who treat sicker patients and work with psychiatrists who prescribe medication. And our training institutions are reporting a persistent trend in the direction of nonmedical candidates outnumbering medical ones.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis: Aparadigm for Clinical Thinking. By André Green; translated by Andrew Weller. London: Free Association Books, 2005, 324 pp., $35.00 paperback.

Elizabeth Brett

Elizabeth Brett

15 Woodland Drive
Woodbridge, CT 06525
Fax: 203-387-1687
E-mail: elizabeth.brett@yale.edu

Elizabeth Brett   Training and Supervising Analyst, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis.

Psychoanalysis: A Paradigm for Clinical Thinking is a collection of André Green's articles published between 1996 and 2001. It offers an introduction to his work for the reader who has not encountered him before and an expansion of familiar arguments for those who have already made his acquaintance. The articles written late in his career extend the ideas he has been developing over a lifetime: the relation of drives to objects; the organization of neurotic and nonneurotic structures; the roles of affect, language, and representation in the mind; the double nature of transference and the place of the third and the negative in psychic functioning. Despite his international prominence and the fame of his “The Dead Mother” (1983), Green's books have been relatively slow to be published in English, and his work is less well known in the United States than the writing of other foreign authors. This is unfortunate, as he proposes a contemporary Freudian perspective of unusual scope and originality.

In his introduction, Green defines psychoanalysis as a form of thinking arising out of and making sense of the clinical encounter. “Clinical thinking forges concepts which express the reasons of the unconscious, the diversity of answers called for by its advances, their transformations into hallucinatory ‘realisations', acting out, somatisations, rationlisations, under the effect of counter-investments, involving disinvestment, etc.” (pp. 10-11). But clinical thinking is subject to the very forces it tries to describe. “To speak of clinical thinking is to allude, in the last instance, to the transformations dictated by anxiety, suffering and pain, to the strategies for denying them, combating them, attempting to get rid of them, but also for trying to transcend them” (p. 11).

Green describes the emergence of clinical thinking in Freud's work on the Dora case and the growing and central recognition by Freud and his contemporaries and successors that clinical thinking was no longer restricted to decoding the patient's psyche but concerned with “the role played by the exchanges between the analysand and the analyst on the outcome of the treatment” (p. 18).
